paragraphs.body: The silver-tooth tiger, Megalodon, dinosaurs, and the Titanoboa cerrejonensis are all animals that we are lucky are not currently living on earth and are extinct. Imagine you take a quick swim in the ocean and are floating on your back, then the next second a large dark shadow forms underneath you and swallows you whole. That could happen if a megalodon, a giant mackerel shark that lived approximately 23 to 3.6 million years ago, were to be alive today. The dodo, Tasmanian tiger, and western black rhinos are animals recently extinct due to humans. But currently, with climate change and overconsumption, a whole list of animals that are endangered and are likely to be extinct in a couple of years is growing larger by the second. Including orangutans, who are warming their spot in the list of endangered animals created by the IUCN.

annotations.text: Imagine you take a quick swim in the ocean and are floating on your back, then the next second a large dark shadow forms underneath you and swallows you whole.

paragraphs.body: In the article “Orangutans: Endangered Animals Spotlight” by Clara Steel Miguelez, Miguelez first introduces us to the three orangutan species, their appearances among gender and species, their diet and feed habits, intelligence, and how crucial of an impact they have in their surrounding ecosystem. But more importantly, she emphasizes her points about the main problems that are affecting the orangutans to the point they are endangered; she points out logging, infrastructure, and palm oil production. Orangutans are a part of the great ape family, which includes humans too, so it concludes that humans share about 97 percent of their DNA with orangutans. Orangutans are very intelligent. I would watch YouTube videos of orangutans called “Orangutan Jungle School,” which included a bunch of young and older orangutans who were displaced from their homes and are rehabilitated by learning life skills and tool use in order to go back to the wild and survive. This video just made me amused at how intelligent orangutans are even at such a young age. They are learning and understanding how to use a bar of soap to clean themselves. They learn how to use saws to cut tree branches to use towards building nests. I also learned so much about orangutans and couldn't help but compare them to humans. They understand when others are hurt. Orangutans are also attached and dependent on their mother until around the age of seven, and even after that, they still drop by and visit their mother. A bond between child and parent is just like humans.

annotations.text: But more importantly, she emphasizes her points about the main problems that are affecting the orangutans to the point they are endangered; she points out logging, infrastructure, and palm oil production.
annotations.comment: Could you write a more complete summary the "points" your reader to the ideas that are important in your own essay? Let's talk about the "art" of summary.

paragraphs.body: To be frank, I've always felt bad when going to zoos and seeing orangutans in their enclosure. We are so similar in many ways, yet we’re both standing on different sides of the glass window. I always kind of mentally noted never going to zoos because I didn't want to see the animals out of their own natural habitat. I wanted them to go back to their homes in the wild, where they truly belong. But what if their homes are no longer theirs?

annotations.text: To be frank, I've always felt bad when going to zoos and seeing orangutans in their enclosure. We are so similar in many ways, yet we’re both standing on different sides of the glass window.
annotations.comment: compelling line. Nice voice here, too.

annotations.text: But what if their homes are no longer theirs?
annotations.comment: This line has heat and energy. Let's explore!

paragraphs.body: Going back to Miguelez’ article about orangutan endangerment, my thoughts about zoos have changed quite a bit. I quite now believe accredited zoos with great conservation efforts are just what the orangutan population needs. According to Miguelez logging, infrastructure and palm oil production displace orangutans, leading to their decline in population. Miguelez writes,Land-use estimates indicate a potential loss of 4,000 Sumatran orangutans by 2030 and a population decrease of 81% by the year 2060.” Basically, Miguelez is predicting an alarming decline in just one of the orangutan species if the habitat destruction caused by logging, infrastructure, and palm oil production attributed by humans continues on without a protest—about not even half but eighty-one percent of the population will decrease by 2060. Not only the Sumatran orangutans are suffering population loss but also the Tapanuli and Bornean orangutans; as Miguelez also writes, “By 2060, researchers estimate a population decline of 83%, with a mere 257 individuals remaining.” and also “The Bornean orangutan population lost over 100,000 individuals between 1999 and 2015.” Miguelez point is that all three orangutan populations are affected by the destruction of their habitats and have declining populations, which rapidly increases their chances of being declared extinct. Now zoos with conservation efforts can come into play and take in orangutans in hopes to maintain and increase their population by enclosureing them and breeding them.

I love to spread nutella on a slice of toast for breakfast in the morning, but one of nutella's main ingredients is palm oil. Nutella claims that the palm oil used is 100% sustainable palm oil certified by the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil (RSPO), and it does not contribute to deforestation. Palm oil is very efficient to grow; it produces more oil with less land used compared to other vegetable oils, so it's less likely companies will give up producing it. The only solution, according to Miguelez, is “the solution to palm oil-induced deforestation thus lies in establishing sustainable practices for plantations to follow and local governments to enforce.” In other words, Miguelez believes that companies should opt for using 100% sustainable palm oil rather than non-sustainable palm oil. Either way, palm oil will always be the orangutan's number one enemy. With loving orangutans comes the responsibility of wanting to do anything in my power to stand clear or engage with anything that affects the orangutans. I have shortened my use of plastic consumption. I try to be aware of products that I buy that might contain palm oil and make sure it is RSPO certified. Miguelez also emphasizes what we could do to help, like supporting a local NGO and purchasing sustainably. Because now, with the rise of social media, overconsumption is now at its high. Recalling that humans and orangutans share 97 percent of DNA, you would think society would deem it important enough to bring extreme awareness to the issues and make a big effort to help orangutans avoid the dangers of extinction. They help their ecosystem and are very important for future research.I would like my grandchildren and future generation to see an orangutan in real time and that they aren't just some wise monkey in a children's book.

annotations.text: The only solution, according to Miguelez, is “the solution to palm oil-induced deforestation thus lies in establishing sustainable practices for plantations to follow and local governments to enforce.” In other words, Miguelez believes that companies should opt for using 100% sustainable palm oil rather than non-sustainable palm oil. Either way, palm oil will always be the orangutan's number one enemy.
